videos.title: Are You Overtraining?
videos.description:
How hard would you need to workout to actually overtrain?

Let’s break it down.

If you do too little in the gym, you won’t grow — that’s called under-training. Do a bit more, and you’ll maintain. A bit more than that, and you’re in the sweet spot for gains.

Keep pushing and you’ll hit functional overreaching, where performance drops for a few days, then rebounds above baseline once you recover.

videos.title: Atopic dermatitis (eczema) - causes, symptoms, diagnosis, treatment, pathology
videos.description: What is atopic dermatitis? Atopic dermatitis, also known as atopic eczema, is a chronic, relapsing skin condition that’s particularly common among young children, but it can also persist well into adulthood. In atopic dermatitis, the immune system overreacts to everyday substances, such as dust and pollen, as if they were dangerous, subsequently triggering inflammation and an extremely itchy rash.

videos.title: Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC): Clinical Nursing Care
videos.description:
What is disseminated intravascular coagulation? Disseminated intravascular coagulation, or DIC, is a life-threatening condition characterized by accelerated, extensive clotting, which causes depletion of platelets and clotting factors and leads to uncontrolled bleeding.

videos.description:

videos.description:
How is carbon dioxide transported in the blood? There are three ways for carbon dioxide to move around in the blood; dissolved directly into the blood plasma; bound to hemoglobin as carbaminohemoglobin; and chemically modified and dissolved in the plasma as bicarbonate ions.

videos.title: Bronchiectasis - physiology, pathology, clinical manifestations, diagnosis, treatment
videos.description:
What is bronchiectasis? With bronchiectasis, “bronchi” refers to bronchi and bronchioles, which are small airways in the lungs, while “-ectasis” means dilation. So, in bronchiectasis, chronic inflammation in the lungs damages the smooth muscle cells and elastic tissue that support these small airways, resulting in their permanent dilation.

videos.title: Spina bifida - physiology, pathology, clinical manifestations, diagnosis, treatment
videos.description:
What is spina bifida? Spina bifida is a neural tube defect where the caudal end of the neural tube doesn’t close properly during early embryonic development. This leads to incomplete formation of the vertebral arches, creating an opening through which the meninges and spinal cord can protrude.

videos.title: Anatomy of the abdominal viscera: Kidneys, ureters and suprarenal glands
videos.description: What are the kidneys, ureters, and suprarenal glands? The kidneys are a pair of bean-shaped abdominal organs, which filter metabolic waste out of the blood in the form of urine. The urine then gets funneled into the urinary bladder by the ureters. Sitting on top of the kidneys, there are the suprarenal glands or adrenal glands. These glands function as a part of the endocrine system by secreting a

videos.title: Primary adrenal insufficiency - pathology, symptoms, diagnosis, treatment
videos.description:
What is primary adrenal insufficiency? Primary adrenal insufficiency occurs when the adrenal glands fail to produce key hormones like glucocorticoids and mineralocorticoids. It can be acute, also known as adrenal crisis, and chronic, which is referred to as Addison disease.

videos.title: Cardiac glycosides: Nursing Pharmacology
videos.description:
What are cardiac glycosides? Cardiac glycosides, also called digitalis glycosides, are medications derived from the foxglove plant, Digitalis purpurea. They are commonly used for the treatment of atrial arrhythmias, such as atrial flutter and atrial fibrillation. In addition, cardiac glycosides can be prescribed for congestive heart failure when other medications fail.

videos.title: How does the morning-after pill prevent pregnancy? - Alison Edelman

In a 2021 study, researchers asked over 500 participants to explain how the morning-after pill worked. Despite these pills’ widespread use, 60% answered incorrectly. Emergency contraceptives are often misunderstood— even the phrase “the morning-after pill” is a misnomer. So, ho

You create your reality with your stories and you can’t choose a different story until you see the one you’re living inside of.

videos.title: What to Do with Triggers
videos.description:
Triggers aren't the cause of your behaviors.
There’s a space between what happens and how you respond.
And inside that space? That’s where your power lives.
With awareness, that space gets a little wider.

videos.title: How Trauma Shows up as Muscle Tension
videos.description:
Trauma often shows up as muscle tension in the body.
Tight shoulders, chronic pain, frozen muscles, flinching, tense shallow breathing — this isn’t your body being “difficult.” It’s your nervous system staying prepared for threat.
Muscles learn to hold when it wasn’t safe to relax. Even after the danger has passed, the body can keep gripping out of habit and protection.
Soft movement, breath, and

videos.title: How to Actually Change
videos.description:
Stopping impulsive reactions isn't easy, in fact it can feel like you're working backwards.  
But your awareness of those impulses is powerful, and a practice of noticing and having curiosity towards those impulses is exactly how you start to change. 

Therapy in a Nutshell and the information provided by Emma McAdam are solely intended for informational and entertainment purposes and are not a su

videos.title: Your Stories Fuel Your Emotions
videos.description:
Our stories don’t just shape our thoughts — they shape our actions.
And over time, our actions shape our outcomes.
There is truth to every story, but sometimes our stories can lead us to see things falsely.
See them for what they are and decide if its helpful or not...
So maybe it's time to ask yourself "What kind of stories do I want to tell?"

videos.title: ADHD and Emotional Stickiness
videos.description:
ADHD often comes with emotional stickiness — emotions don’t just pass through, they linger.

A small comment can replay for hours. Frustration can turn into a full-body state. Once an emotion hits, it can be hard to shift gears or move on, even when you want to.

This isn’t being dramatic or holding a grudge. It’s how the ADHD brain processes emotion, attention, and regulation. When executive func

videos.title: ADHD and Emotional Dysregulation
videos.description:
ADHD isn’t just about focus — it’s deeply connected to emotional dysregulation, and many people carry this into adulthood.
When emotional regulation develops later, adults with ADHD often grow up without the support they needed. Over time, this can shape painful identities like "bad", "dumb", "sensitive."
ADHD often contributes to emotions flooding the nervous system, which can lead to a dysregula

videos.title: Chronic Pain Messaging in the Brain
videos.description:
Pain is felt in the body — but it’s processed in the brain.
There’s acute pain from injury, and neuroplastic pain from a nervous system stuck in danger mode. Neuroplastic pain is real, visible on brain scans, and often doesn’t improve with surgeries or repeated physical treatments.
The good news?
Neuroplastic pain is treatable.
Work by Alan Gordon shows that teaching the brain safety — rather than

videos.title: Medications That Cause Depression
videos.description:
Many commonly prescribed medications can contribute to or worsen symptoms of depression. Things like certain blood pressure medications, hormonal treatments, steroids, sleep medications, and others can affect brain chemistry, energy levels, and mood.

If you started feeling more depressed, flat, or emotionally different after beginning a new medication, it’s worth having a conversation with your p

videos.title: Chronic Pain Management
videos.description:
Many people with chronic pain are given a structural diagnosis — bulging discs, degeneration, arthritis — and from then on, every sensation feels like damage.
But research shows many pain-free people have the same findings.
The diagnosis can train the brain to keep the nervous system on high alert.
Over time, pain becomes predictive — a learned danger signal, not ongoing injury.
The pain is real.

videos.title: ADHD, Emotions and Executive Function
videos.description:
ADHD isn’t just about attention — it impacts emotions and executive function in powerful ways.

Many people with ADHD feel emotions more intensely and have a harder time regulating them in the moment. At the same time, executive function challenges can make it difficult to pause, plan, organize, or follow through — especially under stress.

Support, structure, and compassion can change everything.

videos.title: The Opposite of Motivating
videos.description:
Harsh self-talk puts your nervous system into freeze, making it harder to move, focus, or change. 
Waiting to "feel" like doing something doesn't bring you out of freeze either 

Real motivation comes from safety. 
When you feel supported and safe inside your own body, action comes naturally.

videos.title: Chronic Pain Treatment
videos.description:
One of the most powerful tools we use to calm that system is somatic tracking.
Somatic tracking means gently noticing the sensation in your body without fear, judgment, or the urge to immediately fix it. Instead of bracing against the pain, you observe it with curiosity:
Is it sharp or dull? Does it move? Does it change when I breathe?
Over time, that shift can help retrain the brain and turn the

videos.title: Hormone Imbalances That Look Like Depression
videos.description:
Hormones play a huge role in how we feel emotionally. When they’re out of balance—whether it’s thyroid issues, perimenopause, low testosterone, etc—it can show up as low mood, fatigue, irritability, brain fog, or feeling like you’ve “lost yourself.”

Mental health and physical health are deeply connected. If something feels off, it’s worth getting curious about the whole picture—not just your mind

videos.title: The Fawn State
videos.description:
The fawn response is a survival strategy we don’t talk about enough.
It’s when your nervous system learns that the safest option is to please, appease, and submit in order to avoid conflict or harm.
This happens most when the person you’re meant to connect with… is also a source of harm.
And is often accompanied with dissociation. 
At its core, fawning is your body saying: “If I stay agreeable, I’

videos.title: We Don’t React to Events…
videos.description:
We don’t react to events — we react to the story we tell about them.
The story you tell shapes your emotions, your identity, and your quality of life.
You don’t have to change the facts, Just notice the story… 
and ask: Is this the only way to see this?

videos.title: What is Medical Trauma?
videos.description:
Medical trauma and PTSD happen when experiences with medical care overwhelm your nervous system.
It’s not just about scary events — it can be a painful surgery, repeated hospital visits, or even feeling ignored or unsafe by healthcare providers.
Your body and brain store that stress, creating fear, anxiety, or hypervigilance long after the medical event is over. 

This Interview is from my other Y

videos.title: How You Bias Your Memory
videos.description:
The stories we tell ourselves shape what we notice, remember, and expect.
Stories aren’t inherently bad, but believing they're true all the time isn't helpful.
You don’t have to change your story...Just notice it and ask: Is this story helping me?

videos.title: The Most Actionable Way to Improve Your Therapy
videos.description:
Bringing a notebook to therapy might seem simple, but it can completely change how much you get out of the work. 
When you write things down during or after session, you’re more likely to remember the insights, the tools, and the patterns you’re starting to notice.
Therapy is one hour a week. The real change happens in the other 167 hours. A notebook helps bridge that gap.

videos.title: Getting Stuck in a Trauma Cycle
videos.description:
Trauma doesn’t just happen once—it can become a cycle.
Your nervous system is just trying to protect you using patterns it learned a long time ago, but this can make it feel like you’re stuck in the same reactions, the same emotions, the same patterns in relationships… over and over again.
Narrative Therapy can be really helpful with this. Tell yourself a different story, give yourself a different

videos.title: The Secret Fuel of Social Anxiety
videos.description:
Avoidance feels like relief in the moment—but it’s the secret fuel of social anxiety.
Each time you cancel plans, stay quiet, or leave early, your brain learns: “Good call—that situation wasn’t safe.”
And the next time? The anxiety shows up even stronger.
Avoidance doesn’t solve anxiety—it teaches it to grow.

Thanks to Deb Dobson for this interview! Watch it here:

videos.title: How Trauma Makes us Reactive
videos.description:
Trauma can make you feel more sensitive, more reactive, more easily overwhelmed. 
Little things don’t feel so little anymore—and emotional experiences can start to pile up quickly.
When your nervous system has been through a lot, it doesn’t take much to tip the scale.

videos.title: Exploring Chronic Pain
